text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY — MULTIDISCIPLINARY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Approximately 3 Months Post-Completion of Definitive Concurrent Chemoradiation

---

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Head and Neck Medical Oncology
PARTICIPATING SERVICES: Radiation Oncology, Speech-Language Pathology, Dietetics

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 52-year-old female, never-smoker, of West African descent, who presents for multidisciplinary oncology follow-up approximately 3 months after completing definitive concurrent chemoradiation for HPV-positive (p16+) squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue, staged T2N1M0 (Stage III per AJCC 8th edition conventional staging; Stage I per HPV-positive oropharyngeal staging criteria). She has had restaging PET-CT and MRI of the neck obtained within the past 2 weeks and presents today for results review and ongoing management planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu originally presented roughly 4 months ago with a 3-month history of progressive right-sided odynophagia and a palpable right neck mass. Workup revealed an exophytic, friable 3.2 cm mass at the right base of tongue with a single right level IIA lymph node measuring 2.1 cm on MRI. Biopsy via direct laryngoscopy confirmed invasive squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated, with strong, diffuse block-like p16 positivity in greater than 75% of tumor cells; HPV RNA in situ hybridization was positive for high-risk HPV type 16. Staging PET-CT confirmed FDG-avid disease at the right base of tongue (SUVmax 14.2) and the right level IIA node (SUVmax 9.8) with no distant metastases.

After extensive multidisciplinary tumor board discussion, the patient was recommended for definitive concurrent chemoradiation rather than upfront surgery. She underwent prophylactic PEG tube placement in anticipation of nutritional compromise, then began IMRT using a simultaneous integrated boost (SIB) technique (70 Gy in 35 fractions to the high-risk clinical target volume, with appropriately dosed intermediate- and low-risk fields) with concurrent weekly cisplatin at 40 mg/m2. She completed all 35 fractions of radiation without any treatment interruption, though she required one cisplatin hold in week 5 due to grade 2 nephrotoxicity (creatinine peak 1.3 mg/dL, which recovered to 1.0 mg/dL with aggressive IV hydration) and grade 2 thrombocytopenia. She ultimately received 6 of 7 planned cisplatin cycles, for a cumulative dose of approximately 414 mg/m2 — approximately 83% of the intended dose, which is generally considered oncologically adequate.

The course of treatment was complicated by grade 3 oral mucositis in week 4, requiring a 3-day inpatient admission for IV hydration, electrolyte repletion (hypomagnesemia corrected with IV magnesium sulfate), and initiation of opioid analgesia (PCA with morphine, transitioned to extended-release oxycodone with immediate-release breakthrough dosing). PEG tube feeds were started during that hospitalization at goal rate of Vital 1.5 at 75 mL/hr for 16 hours per day. She also developed grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the right neck, grade 2 dysgeusia, grade 2 xerostomia, and grade 1 fatigue during treatment. She completed radiation without further admissions.

She was seen immediately post-treatment with a total weight loss of 9 kg (12% of baseline) and was fully PEG-dependent at that time. The right level II neck node was no longer palpable on clinical exam after treatment. ECOG performance status at that visit was 2 due to fatigue, pain, and dysphagia. Swallowing rehabilitation was initiated with speech-language pathology.

Restaging imaging was obtained at approximately 10 weeks post-treatment:
- PET-CT showed a complete metabolic response, with no FDG-avid disease at the right base of tongue (SUVmax 2.1, physiologic range) or the right level IIA nodal station (previously FDG-avid node now 0.8 cm fibrotic-appearing, SUVmax 1.8). No new areas of FDG avid disease and no distant metastases.
- MRI neck with and without contrast showed significant interval reduction of the right base of tongue lesion from 3.2 cm to 0.9 x 0.7 cm with decreased enhancement and T2 signal, most consistent with post-treatment fibrosis or scar tissue; the right level IIA node is now 0.8 cm without internal necrosis or pathologic enhancement.

She presents today in clinic for integration of these results and multidisciplinary management planning.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

General: Reports gradual but meaningful improvement in energy and overall functional status since the end of treatment. Fatigue improving but not yet back to baseline. Appetite slowly recovering.

HEENT / Oropharynx: Ongoing odynophagia, significantly improved from the nadir of treatment but still present with swallowing of certain textures. Dry mouth remains bothersome, particularly at night and with meals. Taste described as "still off" with a persistent metallic quality for some foods, though somewhat improved compared to the immediate post-treatment period. No new oropharyngeal pain, no bleeding from the mouth.

Neck: No new palpable lumps. Mild right neck skin tightness noted.

Respiratory: No cough, no hemoptysis, no shortness of breath.

Gastrointestinal: Tolerating pureed/soft foods orally, currently supplementing with PEG tube feeds at night. No nausea or vomiting. Bowel function normal.

Genitourinary: Urine output normal, no dysuria. Creatinine has been stable per labs.

Neurology: No peripheral neuropathy symptoms. No tinnitus reported. Mild right ear fullness, intermittent.

Psychiatric / Social: Patient reports she is "feeling more hopeful" since completing treatment. She is anxious about the MRI finding of a residual 0.9 cm area at the base of tongue, which was discussed extensively by phone after imaging but is being formally addressed today. She is eager to return to her role as a high school music teacher.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 118/72 mmHg, heart rate 78 bpm, temperature 36.8°C, respiratory rate 16/min, oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Weight: 67 kg (9 kg below pre-treatment weight of approximately 76 kg; stable from the post-treatment visit).

General: Well-appearing female in no acute distress. Alert, oriented, conversational.

Head and Neck:
- Skin: Residual grade 1 radiation dermatitis of the right neck with post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation and mild skin thickening, no moist desquamation, no open wounds.
- Oral cavity: Mucositis resolved; oral mucosa moist with mild residual erythema at bilateral buccal surfaces. Salivary pooling reduced consistent with xerostomia. Tongue and floor of mouth without mass or ulceration.
- Oropharynx: Right base of tongue demonstrates post-radiation changes with visible fibrotic mucosal changes and mild edema; no exophytic mass, no ulcerated lesion, no necrosis visible on direct examination. No trismus — mouth opening approximately 4.2 cm.
- Neck: No palpable cervical adenopathy bilaterally. Right level IIA region — previously palpable node is no longer palpable. No supraclavicular adenopathy. No carotid bruit on auscultation.
- Thyroid: Not enlarged on palpation.

Respiratory: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. PEG tube site well healed, no erythema, no granulation tissue, no leakage.
Neurological: Cranial nerves II through XII intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits. Gait normal.
Lymphedema: No clinical lymphedema of the face or neck at this time.

Flexible Nasopharyngoscopy (performed by Radiation Oncology in clinic today): Post-radiation mucosal changes at the right base of tongue; the mucosa appears smooth and fibrotic over the right base of tongue and right tonsillar fossa region. No mucosal ulceration, no raised or exophytic lesion identified. Hypopharynx and larynx without abnormality. Vocal cord mobility symmetric and full bilaterally. Post-radiation edema of the arytenoids, mild, unchanged from prior exam. No pooling of secretions in the pyriform sinuses.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

- CBC: WBC 5.8 x10^9/L (normal differential), Hgb 11.9 g/dL (mild anemia, improving — was 10.8 at end of treatment), Platelets 224 x10^9/L (recovered; nadir was 88 during week 5 of cisplatin).
- BMP: Sodium 138 mEq/L, Potassium 4.1 mEq/L, Chloride 103 mEq/L, Bicarbonate 25 mEq/L, BUN 14 mg/dL, Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (baseline was 0.8; completely recovered from grade 2 nephrotoxicity during treatment), eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73m2.
- Magnesium: 1.9 mg/dL (low-normal; patient on oral magnesium supplementation, to continue).
- TSH: 6.8 mIU/L (elevated — consistent with radiation-induced hypothyroidism; free T4 1.0 ng/dL, low-normal). Prior to radiation, TSH was well controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg/day. Levothyroxine dose will be increased (see Assessment/Plan).
- LFTs: AST/ALT/alkaline phosphatase within normal limits.
- LDH: Normal.
- Albumin: 3.4 g/dL (mildly reduced, consistent with ongoing nutritional recovery).

---

IMAGING RESULTS

PET-CT (obtained approximately 10 weeks post-treatment): Discussed in detail with patient. Complete metabolic response. The right base of tongue demonstrates physiologic FDG activity (SUVmax 2.1), without any discrete FDG-avid mass. The previously hypermetabolic right level IIA lymph node has resolved to a 0.8 cm fibrotic-appearing node with no FDG uptake (SUVmax 1.8). No new FDG-avid cervical, mediastinal, or distant nodal disease. No pulmonary, hepatic, or osseous metastases. Post-radiation inflammatory changes in the pharyngeal mucosa are noted, as expected. Overall, this is an excellent response and represents a complete metabolic response to definitive chemoradiation.

MRI Neck with and without contrast (obtained approximately 10 weeks post-treatment, for anatomic correlation): Significant interval reduction of the right base of tongue lesion. The previously 3.2 cm enhancing mass now measures 0.9 x 0.7 cm with substantially decreased enhancement and T2 signal hyperintensity, consistent with post-treatment fibrosis or scar. Right level IIA node is now 0.8 cm without internal necrosis or pathologic enhancement pattern. No new mucosal lesions, no new adenopathy, no new lesions. The pattern is consistent with a complete or near-complete anatomic response, concordant with the PET-CT findings.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HPV-positive (p16+) right base of tongue squamous cell carcinoma, T2N1M0 — COMPLETE METABOLIC RESPONSE to definitive concurrent chemoradiation.

I reviewed today's imaging in detail with Ms. Osei-Bonsu, along with her husband who accompanied her to this visit. The PET-CT, which is our most functionally informative study, shows a complete metabolic response with no residual FDG-avid disease at either the primary site or the regional lymph node. The MRI corroborates this finding — the residual 0.9 cm lesion at the base of tongue demonstrates features most consistent with fibrosis/scar and shows no pathologic enhancement. This is an outstanding result.

I want to be transparent with the patient about the small residual anatomic lesion on MRI: the vast majority of such lesions in HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC patients represent treatment-related fibrosis rather than viable tumor, particularly when concordantly PET-negative. In the context of our multidisciplinary discussion, the tumor board's consensus is that this lesion is almost certainly post-treatment scar tissue. However, given the size and the small but non-zero possibility of residual viable tumor, the tumor board has recommended direct laryngoscopy with biopsy under general anesthesia to definitively characterize this finding. This will be performed at approximately 5 months post-treatment (approximately 6-8 weeks from now), allowing further maturation of any residual inflammatory changes while not delaying pathologic confirmation unreasonably.

- No adjuvant systemic therapy is indicated at this time. Her imaging response and HPV-positive biology are highly favorable markers. The published literature (RTOG 0129, ECOG-ACRIN 3311) strongly supports the favorable prognosis of complete responders in this patient population.
- She was offered enrollment in a post-treatment surveillance de-escalation research protocol but declined at this time; this will be revisited.
- Surveillance plan per NCCN guidelines for HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC: clinical examination every 1-3 months in year 1, every 2-6 months in year 2, then annually. Annual imaging (MRI or PET-CT) planned at the 1-year and 2-year marks at minimum, with flexible individualization based on clinical course.

2. Residual 0.9 cm right base of tongue lesion on MRI — recommendation for direct laryngoscopy and biopsy under general anesthesia at approximately 5 months post-treatment (4-6 weeks from today). Referral placed to head and neck surgery.

3. Radiation-induced hypothyroidism — TSH elevated at 6.8 mIU/L (up from well-controlled baseline on levothyroxine 75 mcg prior to treatment). This is a recognized late effect of neck irradiation and was anticipated. Levothyroxine dose increased from 75 mcg to 100 mcg daily. Repeat TSH in 6 weeks. Patient counseled that ongoing thyroid monitoring will be required and that late hypothyroidism is common after head and neck radiation.

4. Nutritional status and PEG tube management — Patient is currently using the PEG tube for approximately 50% of her caloric needs, supplementing nighttime enteral feeds with oral intake of pureed and soft foods during the day. Weight is stable at 67 kg (9 kg below pre-treatment weight). Albumin mildly reduced at 3.4 g/dL. Plan to maintain PEG tube for now and reassess removal readiness at the next visit. Dietetics goal: advance oral caloric intake to >75% of needs over the next 4-6 weeks. Continuation of Vital 1.5 via PEG at reduced rate overnight as a safety net. Target for PEG removal: patient consistently meeting >1800 kcal/day orally with stable or improving weight.

5. Swallowing rehabilitation — Patient is working actively with speech-language pathology and has advanced to a pureed diet with soft-solid tolerance. FEES is scheduled for repeat assessment in 4 weeks by SLP. She continues daily tongue base retraction and pharyngeal strengthening exercises (Effortful Swallow, Mendelsohn maneuver), thermal-tactile stimulation, and head-lift exercises (Shaker protocol variant). Goal: advancement to soft mechanical diet within 4-6 weeks, followed by progressive trial of regular diet consistency.

6. Xerostomia and oral hygiene — Grade 1-2 persistent xerostomia. Managing with frequent sips of water, Biotene mouthwash and gel, salivary substitute spray (Numoisyn Liquid), and fluoride trays (ongoing dental follow-up every 3 months given radiation exposure). Consideration of initiating pilocarpine (Salagen) 5 mg TID if xerostomia remains bothersome at next visit — discussed with patient today. Patient deferred initiation for now, preferring to trial conservative measures first.

7. Mild anemia — Hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL, improving from nadir. Iron studies to be drawn today (ferritin, serum iron, TIBC) to assess for iron deficiency as contributing etiology in setting of reduced oral intake. B12 and folate to be checked as well. No transfusion indicated at current level.

8. Hypomagnesemia — Magnesium 1.9 mg/dL, low-normal, likely persisting from cisplatin-related renal magnesium wasting. Continue oral magnesium supplementation (magnesium oxide 400 mg BID). Recheck magnesium with next labs in 6 weeks.

9. Hearing — No new complaints of tinnitus or significant hearing loss reported. A post-treatment audiogram is scheduled in the next 4-6 weeks given cumulative cisplatin exposure (414 mg/m2) and the known risk of delayed cisplatin-associated ototoxicity.

10. BRCA2 germline variant — NGS testing identified a BRCA2 p.D2723H variant (allele frequency 48%, raising concern for germline origin). Patient was counseled about the importance of formal germline genetic counseling and has been referred to the Cancer Genetics clinic. Appointment pending. No change in management until germline status is formally characterized.

11. General survivorship — Patient appears to be psychologically adapting well to the post-treatment period, though she remains understandably anxious about the residual MRI finding. She was reassured extensively today. Resources provided including survivorship care plan documentation, referral to the head and neck cancer survivor support group, and connection with the oncology social worker for additional support as she transitions back to work. She plans to return to teaching in the next 1-2 months as energy and oral function permit.

Current medications:
- Levothyroxine 100 mcg orally daily (increased from 75 mcg today; thyroid function recheck in 6 weeks)
- Oral magnesium oxide 400 mg twice daily
- Biotene oral rinse and gel as needed
- Fluoride trays (1.1% sodium fluoride) nightly
- Extended-release oxycodone and immediate-release oxycodone — currently weaning; patient down to oxycodone immediate-release 5 mg every 8-10 hours as needed for residual oropharyngeal pain; long-acting formulation discontinued this week given improvement
- Vital 1.5 enteral nutrition via PEG tube — continue at reduced overnight schedule as directed by dietetics
- Ondansetron 4 mg as needed for nausea (rarely using)

Return to clinic: In 6 weeks for surveillance examination, repeat labs (CBC, CMP, magnesium, TSH, iron studies), and planning for direct laryngoscopy and biopsy. Head and neck surgery referral placed today.

Visit length: 75 minutes (multidisciplinary follow-up)
Time spent counseling: greater than 50% of visit time

Electronically signed — Head and Neck Medical Oncology Attending
